title: Replacing a basin pop-up waste
subtitle: Swap a stuck or worn pop-up waste assembly on a basin and bath
excerpt: How to remove an old pop-up basin waste and fit a replacement so the plug seats cleanly, the linkage moves freely, and the joint to the trap stays watertight. Covers the back-nut under the basin, the pivot arm, and the lift rod adjustment.

glossaryTerms.definition: A basin waste with a stopper that rises and falls when a lift rod behind the tap is pulled or pushed. The stopper is moved by a pivot arm that links the lift rod to the waste body.
The large brass nut under the basin that clamps the waste body onto the basin underside. Sealed with a rubber washer above the basin and a plumber's putty bedded under the waste flange.

content.text: A
pop-up waste

sticks open or closed after years of use. The seal under the plug fails and the basin drains slowly even with the plug up. The fix is a full swap: a replacement assembly is sold as a unit at any plumbing-merchant counter.
Step 1, isolate and drain

Close the isolating valves on the basin tap pipes if they are fitted. The waste itself does not need a water isolation, but the work under the basin is faster with no risk of a knock-on tap drip. Run the basin tap to clear residual water. Place a bowl under the trap.
Step 2, disconnect the trap

Undo the compression nut joining the trap to the waste tail by hand. Most modern traps are plastic and the nut turns by hand. A brass nut needs the adjustable spanner. Catch the water still sitting in the trap with the bowl. Lower the trap free of the waste tail.
Step 3, undo the pivot arm
The pivot arm enters the waste body through a side nut. Unscrew the nut by hand. Pull the pivot arm out. The plug should now lift free from the top of the basin.
Step 4, undo the back-nut
Reach under the basin to the
back-nut

that clamps the waste body to the basin. Hold the top flange of the waste from above so it does not turn. Unscrew the back-nut anticlockwise with the adjustable spanner. Lift the old waste up out of the basin. Scrape any old plumber's putty off the basin around the hole with a flat screwdriver.
Step 5, fit the new waste body

Run a thin roll of plumber's putty around the underside of the new top flange. Lower the waste into the hole from above. Below the basin, fit the supplied rubber washer, then the back-nut. Tighten the back-nut firmly by hand, then snug a quarter-turn with the spanner. Do not over-tighten; the ceramic basin can crack at the rim. Wipe excess putty off the flange with a damp cloth.
Step 6, fit the pivot arm and plug

Drop the new plug into the waste from above. Slide the new pivot arm into the side hole of the waste body. The ball end of the pivot arm sits into the socket on the underside of the plug. Tighten the pivot arm nut by hand. Lift the plug from above by pushing up on the pivot arm; it should rise smoothly.
Step 7, connect the lift rod

The lift rod comes through the back of the tap. Behind the basin, the rod meets the pivot arm at the linkage plate. Slot the lift rod into one of the holes in the linkage plate. Tighten the small clamp screw. Pull the lift rod up and check the plug rises about 8 mm; this is the height that lets the basin drain freely. Adjust the clamp position on the rod to set the plug height.
Step 8, refit the trap and test

Wrap three turns of PTFE tape around the threads of the waste tail. Refit the trap to the tail and hand-tighten the compression nut. Open the isolating valves and run the tap. Check the trap joint and the waste back-nut with a dry tissue. Tighten by a sixth of a turn at a time on any joint that weeps. Lift and lower the lift rod a few times to confirm the plug rises and seats cleanly.
Variations

On a click-clack waste (a pop-up that opens and closes when the plug is pressed by hand), the linkage and lift rod are not used. The plug body itself has a sprung mechanism. Same back-nut, same putty, no pivot arm to fit. Click-clack wastes need a slow-close spring rather than the lift-rod set-up.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Slow weep from the back-nut after the job
attrs.cause: The rubber washer was not seated, or the plumber's putty was not pressed into the basin underside.
attrs.fix: Drain the trap, slacken the back-nut, lift the waste, refit the putty and washer, retighten. Wipe excess putty before final tightening.

attrs.symptom: The plug does not rise high enough to drain the basin
attrs.cause: The lift rod is clamped at the wrong point on the linkage.
attrs.fix: Loosen the clamp screw and move the linkage attachment one hole closer to the lift rod. Test and re-clamp.

attrs.symptom: The plug will not stay down when pushed
attrs.cause: The pivot arm ball is not seated in the plug socket.
attrs.fix: Lift the plug out. Refit the pivot arm so the ball end sits inside the plug socket before retightening the side nut.
